name: Sarras
description: Sarras, also known as the Spiritual City, is a mystical and highly significant location in Arthurian legend, particularly within the Lancelot-Grail cycle. It is described as a holy city located far to the East, beyond the known world, and is the final destination of the Holy Grail after its adventures in Britain. The city is depicted as possessing immense spiritual power and beauty, representing a connection between the earthly and divine realms. Galahad, Percival, and Bors ultimately sail to Sarras with the Grail, where Galahad is crowned king. He then performs miracles and experiences profound religious visions before his death. The Grail remains in Sarras for a time, bringing prosperity and spiritual enlightenment to its inhabitants. However, after Galahad's death and the subsequent passing of Percival, the Grail ascends to Heaven, leaving Sarras once more as a city among others, though forever marked by its divine association. Its importance lies in representing the culmination of the Grail quest, the attainment of spiritual perfection, and the transient nature of earthly holiness. The city serves as a symbol of divine grace and the ultimate goal of spiritual aspiration, a place where the earthly and celestial converge before the divine withdraws once more.
subtitle: Holy City, Grail's Final Resting Place

keyMyths.title: Galahad's Coronation
keyMyths.description: Galahad is crowned king of Sarras upon arrival, signifying his spiritual purity and divine appointment. He rules with wisdom and justice before his eventual death.

keyMyths.title: The Grail's Ascension
keyMyths.description: After Galahad and Percival's deaths, the Holy Grail ascends to Heaven from Sarras, marking the end of its earthly presence. This symbolizes the transient nature of divine grace.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A major source for Arthurian legends, detailing the Grail quest and Sarras.
sources.text: A major source for Arthurian legends, detailing the Grail quest and Sarras.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A compilation of Arthurian tales, including accounts of the Grail and its destination.
